mnemonics.title: ERA - Entity Relationship Attribute
mnemonics.content: Every Real-world Actor - Remember that ER diagrams model real-world actors (entities), how they relate to each other (relationships), and their properties (attributes)

mnemonics.title: RAP for Cardinality
mnemonics.content: R-A-P = Relationship-Association-Placement: Remember: One (1) looks like a single finger pointing, Many (N/M) looks like many fingers spread out

mnemonics.title: Weak Entity Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Weak = Needs Support: A weak entity always has a double rectangle and is connected via an identifying relationship (double diamond) to its owner entity

mnemonics.title: Attribute Types Memory
mnemonics.content: PED: P=Primary (underlined), E=Derived (dashed/dotted), D=Multivalued (double oval) - Think 'Primary Emphasis on Derived Details'

mnemonics.title: Total Participation Rememberer
mnemonics.content: TOTAL = DOUBLING: Total participation is shown by double lines connecting entity to relationship - both lines must be present for total participation

acronyms.usage: The overall diagram type - shows how data entities relate to each other
Unique identifier for an entity, shown underlined in the entity rectangle
An attribute that references the primary key of another entity, creates relationships between tables
Cardinality where each entity in the relationship corresponds to exactly one entity in the other set
Cardinality where one entity can be associated with many entities in the other set
Cardinality where many entities can associate with many entities in the other set - usually requires a junction table

visualTips.visualization: Think of entities as NOUNS in a sentence - person, place, thing, or event. Represented by a Rectangle (box). Example: Student, Course, Library
Think of attributes as ADJECTIVES describing entities. Represented by Oval/Circle shapes connected to their entity. Example: Student Name, Student ID, Age
Think of relationships as VERBS showing action between entities. Represented by Diamond shape connecting entities. Example: Enrolls-in, Teaches, Borrowed-by
Visualize a WEAK entity as a person holding onto a STRONG entity for support. Shown with double rectangle. Cannot exist without its owner entity.
Think of MULTI = MANY. If an attribute can have multiple values (like phone numbers), draw it as a DOUBLE oval to show it can hold multiple values.
Think of DERIVED = CALCULATED. Values can be computed from other attributes. Shown as DASHED/DOTTED oval. Example: Age can be derived from Date of Birth.
Think of composite as BROKEN DOWN into smaller parts. Drawn as ovals connected to other ovals. Example: Name → First Name + Last Name
Think of TOTAL = COMPLETE involvement. Shown by DOUBLE LINE from entity to relationship - the entity MUST participate in the relationship

analogies.analogy: Think of entities like TYPES OF PEOPLE at a university: Students, Professors, Courses - just like different categories of people in a school
Like a STUDENT ID CARD: The card describes a student (entity) with attributes like Name, Roll Number, Department, Photo - all describing qualities of that student
Like RELATIONSHIPS in a family: 'Father of', 'Married to', 'Brother of' - these describe how people (entities) are connected to each other
Like a CHILD to PARENT: A child (weak entity) cannot exist independently without a parent (owner entity). The child depends on the parent for identity.
Like your AADHAR NUMBER or PASSPORT NUMBER: It's unique to you (entity) - no two people can have the same identification number
Like RELATIONSHIP TYPES in social media: One account can have Many followers (1:N), Many users can Like Many posts (M:N), One person can have One spouse (1:1)
Like BIRTH CERTIFICATE to CHILD: The birth certificate (identifying relationship) helps identify the child (weak entity) through the parents (owner entity)
Like MANDATORY CLASS ATTENDANCE: If every student MUST enroll in at least one course (total participation), there's a double line from Student to 'Enrolls-in' relationship
